agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A global logistics platform adding real-time driver earnings transparency across three jurisdictions with fundamentally different labor classifications, legal requirements, and institutional approval processes. The feature must display base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and deductions — but what each of those terms means, what must be shown, and who must approve the display differs radically by jurisdiction.",
    "bounded_context": "Driver Compensation / Earnings Visibility",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Base Pay", "definition": "The fixed compensation component for a delivery task before any bonuses, tips, or deductions. For employees (CA), this must meet or exceed minimum wage floor. For contractors (Indonesia), this is the agreed-upon task rate." },
      { "term": "Per-Delivery Bonus", "definition": "Variable compensation added on top of base pay, triggered by conditions such as surge pricing, distance thresholds, or performance incentives." },
      { "term": "Tip", "definition": "A voluntary payment from the customer to the driver. Legally must never be counted toward base pay or minimum wage calculations in employee jurisdictions." },
      { "term": "Deduction", "definition": "Any amount subtracted from gross earnings — e.g., platform fees, equipment rental, insurance contributions, tax withholdings. In employee jurisdictions, each deduction must be individually itemized and legally authorized." },
      { "term": "Earnings Breakdown", "definition": "The structured, real-time view of all compensation components for a given pay period or delivery, presented to the driver." },
      { "term": "Driver Classification", "definition": "The legal employment status of the driver in a given jurisdiction: employee (CA under AB5, Germany) or independent contractor (Indonesia). This classification governs which earnings rules, transparency obligations, and approval processes apply." },
      { "term": "Works Council (Betriebsrat)", "definition": "A German co-determination body with legal authority to approve or block changes to compensation display systems under the Betriebsverfassungsgesetz (Works Constitution Act). Their approval is a hard prerequisite to rollout in Germany." },
      { "term": "Pay Period", "definition": "The time window over which earnings are aggregated for display and payment. May differ by jurisdiction and classification." },
      { "term": "Wage Floor", "definition": "The legally mandated minimum compensation rate. Applicable in employee jurisdictions (CA minimum wage, Germany Mindestlohn). The earnings breakdown must make it verifiable that the wage floor was met." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Every earnings breakdown must display exactly four components: base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and deductions. No component may be hidden or merged into another.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Merging or hiding components corrupts the integrity of the earnings record and makes it impossible for drivers or regulators to verify correctness. A breakdown that omits a component is not a breakdown.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any earnings breakdown displayed to a driver, assert that exactly four named line items (base_pay, per_delivery_bonus, tips, deductions) are present and individually valued."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Tips must never be included in or offset against base pay or minimum wage calculations.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a legal requirement in California and a fundamental accounting integrity rule. Violating this means wage theft, which is a legal and financial catastrophe.",
      "testable_assertion": "Assert that the minimum wage compliance check uses (base_pay + per_delivery_bonus) only, and that tips are added after wage floor validation."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "In California, the earnings breakdown must be displayed to drivers — this is not optional. Wage transparency is a legal obligation for employees under AB5.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "California labor law mandates wage transparency for employees. Failure to display is a legal violation, not a product preference.",
      "testable_assertion": "Assert that for any driver with jurisdiction=CA, the earnings breakdown endpoint returns a full breakdown and cannot be disabled or deferred."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "In California, every deduction must be individually itemized with a description and legal authorization reference. A single rolled-up 'total deductions' line is insufficient.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "California Labor Code requires itemized wage statements (Section 226). A lump-sum deduction line violates this statute.",
      "testable_assertion": "Assert that for jurisdiction=CA, the deductions component is an array of individually named deduction items, each with a description and authorization_reference field, never a single aggregate value."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "In Germany, the earnings transparency feature must NOT be rolled out until explicit written approval from the applicable Works Council (Betriebsrat) is recorded in the system.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Under the Works Constitution Act (BetrVG §87), the Works Council has co-determination rights over compensation display systems. Deploying without approval is illegal and exposes the company to injunctions.",
      "testable_assertion": "Assert that the feature flag for jurisdiction=DE cannot be enabled unless a works_council_approval record exists with status=approved and a valid approval_date."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "In Indonesia, the earnings breakdown may be displayed but is not legally required. The feature is opt-in at the platform's discretion.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a business policy decision, not a legal mandate. The platform could choose to show or hide it without legal consequence.",
      "testable_assertion": "Assert that for jurisdiction=ID, the feature can be enabled or disabled via configuration without triggering compliance violations."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "Earnings data must reflect real-time or near-real-time state. A driver must never see stale earnings data without a clear indication of the data's freshness timestamp.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Displaying stale earnings as current is a misrepresentation. In employee jurisdictions this could constitute a wage statement violation. Even in contractor jurisdictions, it erodes trust and creates disputes.",
      "testable_assertion": "Assert that every earnings breakdown response includes a last_updated_at timestamp and that the UI displays it. Assert that data older than the defined staleness threshold is flagged visually."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The sum of (base_pay + per_delivery_bonus + tips - deductions) must equal the net earnings displayed. The breakdown must be arithmetically consistent.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "An earnings breakdown that doesn't add up is corrupt data. This is basic accounting integrity — if the numbers don't reconcile, the system is lying.",
      "testable_assertion": "Assert that for every earnings breakdown, net_earnings == base_pay + per_delivery_bonus + tips - total_deductions, with zero tolerance for rounding drift beyond the jurisdiction's smallest currency unit."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "For employee-classified drivers (CA, DE), the displayed base pay for any pay period must meet or exceed the applicable jurisdiction's minimum wage floor when calculated on an hourly basis.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Displaying earnings that show sub-minimum-wage base pay without flagging it would mean the system is documenting a wage violation. The system must either ensure compliance or alert on violation — never silently display illegal pay.",
      "testable_assertion": "Assert that when base_pay / hours_worked < jurisdiction.minimum_wage_rate, the system raises a compliance alert and flags the breakdown."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-10",
      "rule": "Earnings breakdowns must be displayed in the local currency of the driver's jurisdiction. No cross-currency display without explicit conversion disclosure.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Displaying earnings in the wrong currency is a data integrity failure that makes the breakdown meaningless or misleading.",
      "testable_assertion": "Assert that earnings for CA drivers display in USD, DE drivers in EUR, and ID drivers in IDR, matching the jurisdiction's configured currency."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver in California completes a delivery",
      "when": "They view their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "They see base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and individually itemized deductions (each with description and legal authorization), all in USD, with a freshness timestamp, and the numbers reconcile to the displayed net earnings."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A driver in Germany and the Works Council has NOT approved the feature",
      "when": "The platform attempts to enable earnings transparency for DE jurisdiction",
      "then": "The system refuses to activate the feature and returns an error referencing missing Works Council approval."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A driver in Germany and the Works Council HAS approved the feature (approval record exists)",
      "when": "They view their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "They see the full earnings breakdown in EUR, with deductions itemized per German payroll requirements."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A driver in Indonesia completes a delivery",
      "when": "The feature is enabled for ID jurisdiction",
      "then": "They see a full earnings breakdown in IDR. If the feature is disabled, they see no breakdown and no error."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A California driver's base pay for a pay period calculates to below minimum wage on an hourly basis",
      "when": "The earnings breakdown is generated",
      "then": "The system raises a compliance alert to operations and flags the breakdown — it does not silently display sub-minimum-wage earnings as normal."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "Any driver in any jurisdiction views their earnings breakdown",
      "when": "Tips are present",
      "then": "Tips appear as a separate line item and are never included in the base pay or wage floor calculation."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "The earnings data source experiences latency or is temporarily unavailable",
      "when": "A driver requests their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "The system either shows the last known data with a visible staleness warning and timestamp, or shows an explicit 'data unavailable' state — never shows stale data as if it were current."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "Any earnings breakdown is displayed",
      "when": "The components are summed",
      "then": "base_pay + per_delivery_bonus + tips - total_deductions equals the displayed net_earnings exactly (within the smallest currency unit)."
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A driver relocates from Indonesia to California mid-pay-period", "expected_behavior": "Driver classification and transparency rules must switch to CA rules from the effective date of reclassification. Earnings before reclassification follow ID rules; earnings after follow CA rules. The pay period may need to be split." },
    { "scenario": "Works Council approval in Germany is revoked after feature is live", "expected_behavior": "The feature must be immediately disabled for DE jurisdiction. Any cached or queued earnings breakdowns must not be served. The system must treat revocation as a hard stop, not a graceful degradation." },
    { "scenario": "A delivery spans a jurisdiction boundary (e.g., pickup in one region, dropoff in another)", "expected_behavior": "Earnings rules should follow the driver's home jurisdiction classification, not the delivery geography. Escalate to legal if cross-border deliveries create conflicting obligations." },
    { "scenario": "A customer modifies or retracts a tip after the driver has already seen it in their breakdown", "expected_behavior": "The breakdown must update to reflect the revised tip amount with a clear audit trail. The driver must see the adjustment as a distinct line item (e.g., 'tip adjustment'), not a silent modification to the original tip line." },
    { "scenario": "Deductions exceed gross earnings, resulting in negative net pay", "expected_behavior": "The system must display the negative net earnings honestly, never clamp to zero. In employee jurisdictions (CA, DE), this likely indicates an illegal deduction situation and must trigger a compliance alert." },
    { "scenario": "Multiple deliveries completed within seconds of each other", "expected_behavior": "Each delivery must have its own independent earnings breakdown. Breakdowns must never merge or double-count components across deliveries." },
    { "scenario": "California driver has zero tips and zero bonuses for a period", "expected_behavior": "The breakdown must still display all four components — tips and bonus shown as $0.00, not omitted. Omitting zero-value lines violates the itemization requirement." },
    { "scenario": "Currency rounding causes a 1-cent discrepancy between component sum and displayed net", "expected_behavior": "Rounding must be applied consistently using banker's rounding (or jurisdiction-mandated method). A rounding adjustment line may be added if needed, but the displayed total must always reconcile." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "High. Non-compliance in California exposes the platform to class-action wage theft lawsuits (penalties of $50-$4,000 per violation per employee under LC §226). Germany violations risk injunctions and fines. Correct implementation builds driver trust, potentially reducing churn and recruitment costs (driver acquisition costs $300-$1,500 per driver in competitive markets).",
    "operational": "Moderate-to-high. Requires jurisdiction-aware feature flagging, Works Council approval workflows for Germany, real-time earnings calculation pipelines, and compliance alerting infrastructure. Ongoing maintenance includes tracking minimum wage rate changes across jurisdictions.",
    "user": "High positive impact for drivers — earnings transparency is the #1 driver satisfaction factor in gig economy platforms. In CA, it fulfills a legal right. Across all jurisdictions, it reduces payment disputes and support tickets.",
    "risk": "Critical legal risk if CA itemization or wage floor rules are violated. Critical governance risk if Germany feature launches without Works Council approval. Moderate reputational risk if earnings data is stale or inconsistent. Low legal risk in Indonesia but reputational risk if the feature is available elsewhere but not to ID drivers.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers (California)", "impact": "Direct beneficiaries — receive legally mandated wage transparency. Incorrect implementation means their legal rights are violated." },
      { "group": "Drivers (Germany)", "impact": "Blocked until Works Council approves. If rolled out prematurely, it creates a co-determination violation affecting all German employees." },
      { "group": "Drivers (Indonesia)", "impact": "Optional beneficiaries. If feature is withheld while other regions get it, may perceive inequity." },
      { "group": "Works Council (Germany)", "impact": "Must be formally engaged as an approval authority. Bypassing them is a legal and relationship catastrophe." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance team", "impact": "Must validate itemization rules per jurisdiction, maintain minimum wage rate tables, and define deduction authorization standards." },
      { "group": "Finance/Payroll", "impact": "Must ensure earnings calculation engine produces data that reconciles with actual payroll disbursements. Discrepancies create audit findings." },
      { "group": "Platform Operations", "impact": "Must monitor compliance alerts for sub-minimum-wage situations and respond to them. New operational workflow required." },
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Indirect — tip transparency may affect tipping behavior. Tip retraction workflows must be handled gracefully." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 52,
    "reasoning": "The spec is a single sentence with three jurisdiction bullets. Critical details are unspecified: what constitutes 'real time' (per-delivery vs. per-period?), what deduction types exist, how hours_worked is tracked for wage floor calculation in a delivery-based model, whether Germany drivers are employees or have a hybrid classification, and what the Works Council approval workflow looks like. The legal requirements for CA and DE are well-established in law but the spec doesn't reference them — I'm inferring obligations from domain knowledge, not from stated requirements. Multiple ambiguities remain unresolved.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Tips must be separated from base pay — this is legally unambiguous in CA",
      "Works Council approval is a hard prerequisite in Germany — this is statutory",
      "Earnings breakdown must be arithmetically consistent — basic accounting integrity",
      "CA requires itemized deductions — Labor Code §226 is explicit"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "What 'real time' means — per-delivery instant view vs. rolling pay period aggregate",
      "German driver classification — spec says employees under AB5 for CA but doesn't explicitly state DE classification",
      "How hourly wage floor is calculated in a per-delivery compensation model — hours_worked tracking is undefined",
      "Whether Indonesia contractors should see deduction itemization at all (they may have platform fees, not 'deductions' in the employee sense)",
      "Tip retraction/adjustment rules — not mentioned in spec but critical to real-time accuracy"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "German drivers are classified as employees (standard for logistics in Germany, but not stated in spec)",
      "Real time means the breakdown updates within the same session as delivery completion, not batch/end-of-day",
      "The platform already has a jurisdiction-aware feature flagging system or will build one",
      "Minimum wage calculation requires hours_worked data, which the platform tracks or can derive",
      "The Works Council approval is a one-time gate per feature, not per-driver or per-update"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec does not define what 'real time' means for earnings display. Per-delivery instant breakdown vs. rolling pay-period aggregate have very different technical and domain implications. Per-delivery is simpler but may not show the full picture for wage floor compliance (which is calculated over a pay period, not per delivery).",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Legal/Compliance", "Engineering"],
      "options": ["A: Per-delivery breakdown shown immediately after each delivery, with a separate pay-period summary view", "B: Rolling pay-period aggregate updated after each delivery", "C: Both views available"],
      "recommendation": "Option C — both per-delivery and pay-period views. Per-delivery satisfies the 'real time' ask; pay-period is required for wage floor compliance verification in CA and DE."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec does not confirm German driver classification. AB5 is explicitly cited for CA, but Germany is only mentioned in the context of Works Council. If German drivers are independent contractors (unlikely but possible in some platform models), the Works Council requirement and wage floor rules would not apply the same way.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers (Germany)", "Legal/Compliance", "Works Council"],
      "options": ["A: Assume German drivers are employees (standard for logistics in DE)", "B: Require explicit classification confirmation from legal before defining DE rules"],
      "recommendation": "Option A with validation — assume employee classification as this is the norm for German logistics, but flag for legal confirmation before implementation begins."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No definition of what deduction types exist on the platform. Without knowing the deduction taxonomy, it is impossible to define complete itemization rules. CA requires each deduction to have a legal authorization reference — which deductions does the platform currently apply?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers (California)", "Legal/Compliance", "Finance/Payroll"],
      "options": ["A: Define a deduction taxonomy as part of this feature's design phase", "B: Pull the deduction list from the existing payroll system"],
      "recommendation": "Option B — the deduction types must already exist in the payroll system. Extract and codify them as part of the domain model before building the display layer."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
